note_data.title: Transport in plants
keyPoints: **Xylem** transports water and mineral salts from roots to leaves in one direction only
**Phloem** transports sucrose and amino acids from leaves to all parts of the plant in both directions
Xylem vessels are made of dead cells with no end walls and lignified walls for support
Phloem consists of living sieve tube elements with companion cells that provide energy
**Transpiration** is the loss of water vapour from leaves through stomata
Transpiration creates a transpiration stream that pulls water up through xylem vessels
Root hair cells increase surface area for absorption of water by osmosis
Guard cells control the opening and closing of stomata to regulate water loss
Translocation is the transport of sucrose from sources (leaves) to sinks (roots, fruits)

definitions.definition: The loss of water vapour from leaves through stomata
The transport of sucrose and amino acids in phloem from sources to sinks
A cell that increases the surface area of roots for absorption of water and mineral salts
A tube made of dead cells with lignified walls that transports water and mineral salts
A cell that controls the opening and closing of stomata

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give similarities AND differences between xylem and phloem
Give a sequence of events or detailed account of structure

modelAnswer.question: Explain how water moves from the soil into a root hair cell and then up to the leaves. (6 marks)
modelAnswer.answer: Water moves from soil into root hair cell by osmosis because the cell has a lower water potential than the soil. Water then moves across the root cortex by osmosis from cell to cell. Water enters the xylem vessels in the centre of the root. Transpiration from the leaves creates a transpiration stream. This pulls water up through the xylem vessels due to cohesion between water molecules. Water reaches the leaves and evaporates from mesophyll cells before diffusing out through stomata.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Osmosis into root hair cell
Mark 2: Lower water potential in cell

commonMistakes: Never say water is 'sucked up' — always say water is 'pulled up by transpiration stream'
Students confuse xylem and phloem functions — xylem only transports water, phloem only transports food
Don't say stomata 'breathe' — say stomata allow gas exchange
Never say root hairs 'suck in' water — always use 'absorption by osmosis'
Students forget that xylem vessels are dead cells while phloem sieve tubes are living
examinerTips: This topic appears in every Paper 2 — especially transpiration experiments
Always mention 'osmosis' when describing water movement into roots
Draw clear diagrams of xylem and phloem cross-sections with labels
Link transpiration rate to environmental factors like temperature and humidity
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Cross-section of root showing epidermis with root hair cells, cortex, and central vascular bundle containing xylem and phloem. Stem cross-section showing vascular bundles with xylem vessels (thick walls) and phloem (thin walls with companion cells).
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — they asked students to draw four adjacent xylem vessel elements and label the cell wall, requiring continuous thin lines with elements touching
N 2022 P1 Q5 (3 marks) tested cohesion-tension theory — students had to explain how water moves up xylem vessels in a continuous column using cohesion, adhesion and transpiration pull
M/J 2021 P2 Q2 (5 marks) required a plan diagram of root cross-section with xylem labeled — common errors included drawing individual cells instead of tissue regions
N 2023 P1 Q7 (6 marks) combined transpiration and translocation, asking students to compare transport in xylem and phloem including direction, materials transported and energy requirements

workedExample.question: Explain how water moves up xylem vessels to leaves in a continuous column (3 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the three key mechanisms - cohesion between water molecules, adhesion to vessel walls, and transpiration creating the driving force
Step 2: Explain cohesion - water molecules stick together due to hydrogen bonding, forming an unbroken column
Step 3: Explain adhesion - water molecules stick to the cellulose walls of xylem vessels, preventing the column from breaking
Step 4: Explain transpiration pull - water evaporation from leaves creates tension that pulls the entire water column upwards
workedExample.answer: Water molecules are held together by cohesion due to hydrogen bonding between molecules. Water molecules also show adhesion to the cellulose cell walls of xylem vessels. Transpiration from leaves creates tension that pulls the continuous water column upwards through the xylem.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for cohesion/hydrogen bonding between water molecules; 1 mark for adhesion between water and xylem walls; 1 mark for transpiration creates pull/tension on water column

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused xylem and phloem functions' — always specify that xylem transports water and minerals upwards only, while phloem transports sugars in both directions

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often drew individual cells instead of vessel elements when asked for xylem diagrams' — remember xylem vessels are made of dead, hollow vessel elements joined end-to-end
Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates frequently omitted the role of transpiration in creating the driving force' — always mention that transpiration pull is essential for water movement up tall plants
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw four adjacent xylem vessel elements showing: continuous thin lines using available space, each element as a hollow tube touching at least two others, one element drawn larger than the others, double lines representing thick lignified cell walls, and a clear label line pointing to 'cell wall'. The diagram should show the elements are joined end-to-end with no cross-walls between them.
note_data.quickRecap: Xylem transports water and minerals from roots to leaves through dead, hollow vessel elements joined end-to-end. Water moves up by cohesion-tension theory: hydrogen bonding creates cohesion between water molecules, adhesion sticks water to vessel walls, and transpiration from leaves pulls the continuous water column upwards. Phloem separately transports sugars bidirectionally through living sieve tube elements.
